articles.title: Free Electricity for Sunny Weekend Chores
articles.summary: In Britain, families might get free or cheaper electricity for doing laundry or running appliances on sunny weekends. This is because when it's sunny, solar panels make lots of extra electricity, and not many people are using it. The National Energy System Operator (NESO) has a new plan to stop this extra power from going to waste. If you have a smart meter and your energy company joins, you could be rewarded for using electricity at these times. Rewards might include free power, lower bills, or points for gift cards. This helps use clean energy better and keeps the electricity system strong during summer when demand is low.

articles.title: A Former Leader Warns About UK Safety
articles.summary: A former leader of NATO, Lord George Robertson, says the UK's safety is in danger. He thinks the government, led by Sir Keir Starmer, is not doing enough to protect the country. Lord Robertson says the government is being too relaxed about defense and not spending enough money on it. He gave a speech where he warned that Britain is not safe and needs to invest more in defense. The government says it is spending a lot of money on defense, but Lord Robertson disagrees. He believes that too much money is going to welfare instead of defense. This is a big debate about how to keep the UK safe.

articles.title: How a Movie Star Helped Ballet and Opera Sell More Tickets
articles.summary: Timothée Chalamet is a famous actor from Hollywood. He said something that made people talk about ballet and opera. He said that no one cares about these art forms anymore. This made many people upset, but it also helped the Royal Ballet and Opera in London. Their boss, Alex Beard, thanked Chalamet because more people started buying tickets after his comments. On Instagram, their post got millions of likes and shares. Other places, like the Seattle Opera, also used Chalamet's words to offer discounts on tickets. This story shows how a simple remark can bring attention to the arts and help them grow.

articles.title: Greek Police Use Migrants to Push Others Back at Border
articles.summary: In Greece, police have been using migrants to push other migrants back across the border with Turkey, according to a BBC investigation. These migrants, called mercenaries, are hired by police officers to force people back without letting them ask for help or safety. Witnesses say the mercenaries hurt people by beating them, taking their things, and even attacking them. The Greek prime minister says he didn't know about this, and the police haven't answered questions. Pushbacks, which mean sending migrants back without a fair process, are usually against international law. The border is in a place called Evros River, where many migrants try to enter Greece and the European Union. The mercenaries are often from countries like Pakistan, Syria, and Afghanistan, and they get rewards like money or phones from the people they push back.

articles.title: How Presidents and Money Can Mix: A Story About Rules
articles.summary: This article talks about how presidents in the past tried to keep their money separate from their job to be fair. For example, Harry Truman and George W. Bush made sure they didn't use their position to make money. But now, President Donald Trump's family is doing business deals while he is president. His sons, Eric and Donald Jr., are expanding their real estate business to other countries and getting into new things like cryptocurrencies and drone companies. Some people worry this could be a problem because it might look like they are using their dad's job to make money. The White House says there is no issue, but experts think it's important to keep the president's work and family money apart to protect democracy. This could set an example for future presidents.

articles.title: Hungary's New Leader Brings Hope After Long Rule
articles.summary: In Hungary, a country in Europe, there was a big election where people voted to choose their leaders. For many years, Viktor Orban was the prime minister, but now he has lost the election. The new prime minister is Peter Magyar, who wants to make changes quickly. He plans to reverse some of the rules that Orban made during his 16-year rule. Many Hungarians are happy and celebrating in the streets of Budapest, the capital city. This change could affect other countries around the world because Hungary is part of Europe and has friends and enemies. The election had high voter participation, with millions of people voting to support the opposition party called Tisza. This shows that democracy is working in Hungary, and people want a new direction.
